draft.introduction: In Book II of De Rerum Natura, Lucretius introduces the clinamen (swerve)—the unpredictable slight deviation of atoms from their downward path. This swerve breaks the chain of deterministic causation and grounds the possibility of free will.

translation.original_ref: Lucretius, De Rerum Natura II.216–235 (Bailey, OCT)
translation.text: When atoms are carried straight down through void by their own weight, at quite uncertain times and uncertain places they swerve a little from their course, just so much as you might call a change of direction. If they were not apt to swerve, all would fall downward like drops of rain through the depth of the void, and no collision would occur, nor any blow be produced among the atoms. Thus nature would never have produced anything.
translation.original_text: Illud in his quoque te rebus cognoscere avemus, corpora cum deorsum rectum per inane feruntur ponderibus propriis, incerto tempore ferme incertisque locis spatio depellere paulum.

translator_notes.note: The clinamen (swerve) is Lucretius’s term for what Epicurus called parenklisis. It has no external cause—it occurs at no fixed time or place. Some modern interpreters see in it an anticipation of quantum indeterminacy, though this comparison should not be pressed too far.
